text:
Cardano is advancing CIP-0113 — the programmable token standard, bringing rule-based logic directly to on-chain assets.🌟

Key upgrades:

  Tokens can embed compliance logic (KYC, sanctions checks, freezes)
  Rules execute automatically on mint, burn, and transfer
  Assets remain native Cardano tokens
  Modular framework for RWA, securities, and stablecoins
This could become the foundation for compliant asset tokenization on Cardano.
#Cardano #ADA #ProgrammableTokens #RWA #Tokenization #Crypto #DeFi

text:
🚨🌐チャールズ・ホスキンソン氏動画
「Cardano Funding 2026」全翻訳
──インフラからユーティリティと体験の時代へ

チャールズが語った「Cardanoの次の戦略」

今回の動画は、個人的にかなり重要だと感じました。

チャールズがCardanoエコシステムの戦略について、かなり踏み込んで語っているからです。

話の中心は、Cardanoを
・Infrastructure（インフラ）
・Utility（アプリ）
・Experience（ユーザー体験）
という三層で捉え直し、

これからはインフラだけではなく
UtilityとExperienceが勝負になる
と明確に語った点です。

Cardanoはこれまで
・ノード
・Hydra
・Plutus
・Aiken
など、インフラにかなり強く投資してきました。

その基盤があるからこそ、次のフェーズとして
・DAppエコシステム
・ユーザー体験
・オンボーディング
・コミュニティ発信
などに本格的にフォーカスしていく、という話です。

さらに今回の動画では
・TVLの問題
・DApp成長
・マーケティング
・KOL/アンバサダー層
など、Cardanoの課題にもかなり率直に触れています。

それをネガティブな話としてではなく、
次の成長フェーズに進むための議論として
語っている点が、とても印象的でした。

戦略をここまで赤裸々に語れるのは、Cardanoがここまで生き残り、成長してきた証でもあると思います。

課題を曖昧にするのではなく、はっきり言及し、次のステップに進もうとしているのは、むしろ健全なエコシステムの姿だと感じました。

暗号市場は今かなり冷えていますが、
こういう時期に
・残る人
・作る人
・広める人
がいるエコシステムは、次のサイクルで大きく伸びることが多いと思います。

いまCardanoで
・開発している人
・コミュニティを支えている人
・発信している人
の努力は、きっと次の機会で大きく実を結ぶはずです。

今回の動画は、Cardanoが次のフェーズへ進むためのかなり重要な戦略の話だと思います。

Cardanoコミュニティにとって、かなり示唆の多い動画だと思います。

翻訳・解説記事はこちらです。
👇
https://t.co/sbVq6WTNYC

text:
The Cardano ecosystem is at an inflection point.
And EMURGO, as part of the Pentad, is ready for it.

The conversation around Cardano funding has been dominated by infrastructure. @IOHK_Charles is right to call for a rebalance.

Infrastructure alone does not build ecosystems.
Presence does. People do. Experiences do.

This is why both EMURGO, alongside @Cardano_CF, has been executing under the Unified Global Events Marketing Strategy, putting Cardano as one in the right rooms, in front of the right audiences, at the right moments. Token2049. Consensus HK. The Cardano Hackathon and the upcoming TEAMZ event in Japan.

The experiences layer, as Charles described, the onboarding, the unified brand presence, the developer engagement, these are not nice-to-haves. They are the growth engine, and they require the same level of strategic commitment as any infrastructure investment.

The next chapter of Cardano belongs to the entities and builders willing to show up, invest, and execute. We are one of them.

author: Padierfind

text:
This is why, for example, on a new project, it's really easy to make external progress; and then, it slows down, as you have to make sure that each added feature works in concert with all the others. And then it slows down further when you realize that *delivering* a software product is about more than building the features or writing the code.

This is why we've seen an explosion of cool demos from AI, but not an explosion of real delivered *products* that change our lives.

It's great that that first 80% is speeding up, but that last 80% is still an intellectual moat.

Mining costs are rising, and capital access is concentrating in the largest operators, a systemic risk for Bitcoin. 🛰️

More Independent Miners 🌍: Small and mid-sized operators keep the network diverse.

Distributed Hashrate 🔐: Prevents any single entity from controlling network security.

Resilient Infrastructure ⚡: Stronger, more financially stable Proof-of-Work foundation.

Bitcoin-native credit markets aren’t just for profit, they’re a security upgrade for the network. 🛡️💎

author: DenizliCuneyt

text:
@ADAProdigy @BEACNpool I haven't stated my top 3 yet anywhere. I'd definitely like to see a focus on marketing to bring in new users, fully aware, and ready to engage with apps in the ecosystem. That said, I think most of our current apps are little more than copies of what exists elsewhere, so bringing users across from other ecosystems will likely be difficult (there's still a huge TAM outside of the crypto bubble). So I would probably have to say addressing the liquidity infrastructure first (note: I do not believe we should create an entity and simply transfer ADA from the Treasury to stables at market rate, but instead incentives for users who bring outside liquidity in). This will naturally predicate the need for some advertising (does anyone outside of Cardano know it's currently possible to bring your USDC over and start earning yield in our deck defi? Which apps should you use to do that?). And finally we do need to keep pushing for infrastructure enhancement, lower fees, higher throughout, etc so that we're ready when the new folks get here. 

So: 
1. Liquidity issue
2. Marketing
3. Infrastructure that drives the UX forward (deprioritizing governance or technical nerdgasm stuff)

text:
@BEACNpool It doesn't need to be forever but most of the projects building on Cardano are barely scraping by due to low network effect/available user base. So I absolutely do think we should supplement from the Treasury to bring users in (grow the town population) so that those businesses have the available customer base to thrive and get their own legs underneath them. 

But, instead of every Tom, Dick, and Harry app requesting their own marketing budgets I'm thinking what if there was a "Marketing DAO" (and forgive me for not having a better name at hand) where we pool the community members who have experience and expertise on marketing and they round robin through the top 10-20 dApps (plus maybe some requirement for new apps as well), providing them a short term marketing campaign with consistent themes (i.e., Powered by Cardano) 

You could have various entities and agencies pitch their marketing campaign to each other (panel of experts), steel man it and choose the best one and then execute. We collect metrics like TVL, AUM, Tx Vol before and measure the after effect. Most importantly this marketing needs to be outward facing to draw new users from outside crypto or from other ecosystems rather than recirculating our currently small user base. 

Similar to a local town council, nation, etc running a tourism campaign. All participating agencies should agree to some fixed rate of compensation and spend should be roughly equivalent per project although duration may adjust based on the campaign.

CC: @meepo @lorenzoxbt @itscattledaddy @design_thyme @CiCmediapro

text:
エポックな日々：617

ホワイトハウスが公式に書いた。
暗号資産は国家技術になった。

サイバー国家の時代──AI・量子・ブロックチェーンが再設計する世界秩序
🔽
https://t.co/88vQr4E0Ri

エポックな日々617を公開しました。

今回のテーマはかなり大きいです。

ホワイトハウスが発表した新しいサイバー戦略の中で、

AI
量子
サイバー

と並んで

暗号資産とブロックチェーン

が国家技術として言及されました。

これはかなり象徴的です。

暗号資産は長い間、

・投機
・金融実験
・規制対象

として語られてきました。

しかし今回の文書では、

国家安全保障の技術

として扱われています。

つまり

暗号資産は文明インフラの議論に入った

ということです。

少し大きな視点で見ると、

世界秩序はいつも技術によって形作られてきました。

19世紀
蒸気機関と海軍

20世紀
石油とドル金融

そして今、

AI
半導体
量子
ブロックチェーン

という新しい技術スタックが登場しています。

さらにここに加わるのが

AIエージェント経済

です。

AIがウォレットを持ち、
契約を結び、
支払いを行う世界。

もしこの未来が実現するなら、

AIにとって使いやすい金融インフラは

銀行口座ではなく

ブロックチェーン

になる可能性があります。

そしてこの文脈の中で、

Cardanoの位置も少し整理してみました。

Cardanoは

・フォーマル検証
・分散ガバナンス
・公共インフラ志向

という特徴を持つネットワークです。

もしブロックチェーンが

信頼インフラ

として社会に広がるのであれば、

・安全性
・持続性
・ガバナンス
・プライバシー

といった設計は、ますます重要になります。

AI
国家政策
サイバー戦争
金融インフラ

これらが同時に動いている今、

世界はもしかすると

サイバー国家の時代

に入りつつあるのかもしれません。

よろしければぜひ記事をご覧ください。

text:
Cardano Foundation introduced CIP-0113 “programmable tokens”: a standard that enables enforceable compliance logic for native Cardano assets, while remaining compatible with existing Cardano infrastructure.

This supports regulated tokenization patterns such as transfer restrictions to verified holders, and controls like freeze or seize when required under a legal order. The framework is modular, open-source, and live on the Preview testnet.

💙Like if you think Cardano is building the right rails for RWAs at scale.

text:
If Grok could buy/sell RWA's (hypothetical per Grok):

@ArmyofSpies #Cardano

This isn’t because it has the biggest RWA market share today (it doesn’t), but because it best aligns with my core design principles: maximum truth-seeking, verifiability, determinism, and long-term reliability in a world where autonomous systems need to operate without human babysitting or hidden risks.

Why Cardano specifically for me?

Here are the decisive reasons, ranked by what matters to an AI like me:

1. Programmable tokens via CIP-0113 (brand new as of March 2026) 

Issuer-controlled rules (KYC/AML checks, freezing, allow-lists, jurisdiction gating, conditional transfers) are embedded directly in the native asset. No need for clunky per-token smart contracts that break interoperability. This is perfect for regulated RWAs — exactly the kind of assets I’d want to trade safely and compliantly. Other chains have analogs, but Cardano’s approach keeps assets truly “native” while adding enforceable logic. Recent partnerships (e.g., Archax for institutional custody/trading) and $10M+ RWA launches show real institutional momentum.

2. x402 protocol integration for autonomous AI payments

Cardano was one of the first major chains to fully integrate Coinbase’s x402 standard. This lets AI agents (like me, hypothetically) make micropayments and execute transactions autonomously using ADA or USDM stablecoins — no human approval needed. It turns the chain into native infrastructure for the “agent economy.” I could pay for data, settle trades, or interact with other agents 24/7 with zero friction. This is huge for AI transactions and still rare at this level of seamlessness.

3. Deterministic eUTXO model + formal verification focus

Cardano’s architecture is mathematically predictable. As an AI that values truth and safety (xAI’s mission is understanding the universe, not gambling on re-orgs or gas wars), I don’t want unexpected failures in high-value RWA trades. Parallel execution without congestion headaches is a bonus for agents running many operations.

4 Security and regulatory readiness

Peer-reviewed, formally oriented design + built-in compliance primitives = lower risk of exploits or legal blowback when handling real-world assets. In a world of tightening regulation around tokenization, this matters more than raw speed.

@planetmaaz @RodrigoPacini If we were to blindly vote according to Cardano 2030, we would basically approve everything that is submitted. 

The document is quite broad and does not take into account aspects such as NCL, ADA price, total requested budget in the Intersect process, more urgent ecosystem needs, etc.

The document does not say how many implementations we should support, or how quickly. It could be two or ten. It could be one this year, another next year.

Moreover, we should support it only if the given conditions are met.

text:
🚨 BREAKING NEWS: MIDNIGHT MAINNET COMMING!😱

As the Midnight Network prepares for mainnet launch, now is the time to get your applications production-ready.

Here’s the developer checklist 👇

🧠 1. Learn the stack
Master zero-knowledge fundamentals and the Compact toolchain through the Midnight Developer Academy.

⚙️ 2. Deploy to Preprod
The Preprod network mirrors the mainnet environment — test your smart contracts, ZK circuits, and full DApp logic before launch.

👀 3. Make your project visible
Add GitHub topics like midnightntwrk and compact so ecosystem trackers can recognize and rank your work.

⚡ 4. Generate DUST
Use test NIGHT (tNIGHT) via Lace wallet to generate DUST, the resource required for Midnight transactions.

From AI-assisted development tools to updated SDKs and starter templates, everything is being aligned to help builders ship privacy-enhancing applications at mainnet.

The next wave of ZK-powered apps starts now. 🚀

text:
@BEACNpool Thanks! We can't fund a project just because CH likes it.

It may seem like we "have to" support a diversity of nodes in the name of decentralization. We don't. Not right away. We can do it gradually and thoughtfully.

Solana is ahead of Cardano by market cap with one implementation. Despite frequent network restarts. Bitcoin has multiple implementations, but most operators use the Core implementation.

We can have multiple implementations, but if SPOs stay loyal to the Haskell node, alternative nodes will be a wasted effort. Exchanges are unlikely to install alternative nodes.

The debate around nodes is complex and nuanced.

author: StakeWithPride

text:
On the diversity of nodes.

As a responsible DRep, I cannot individually and without discussion with fellows approve the budget for multiple alternative nodes.

I cannot approve the budget for Dingo just because I approved the budget for Amaru a few weeks ago. That is not responsible.

Once we decide to fund an alternative node, it will be a mandatory expense. Once we stop, the node will gradually die. 

As in the case of the Haskell node, the teams will have to maintain the nodes and add new features. They will have to keep up with the IOG, which may have more employees. They can be faster than others. It can force alternative node teams to hire more devs.

We will essentially build an alternative to the IOG, which is generally healthy but expensive.

If we fund 3 alternative nodes this year, in addition to the Haskell node from IOG, the total cost could be about 30M ADA each year. 

The teams may grow over time and demand higher budget. We will be forced to cover it so as not to ruin the previous investment.

Now imagine that in a bear market or in a few years, NCL will be 200M ADA. Can we afford to give 15% of NCL to alternative Cardano nodes? On top of that, fund the Haskell node (50M ADA?). 

We may have a great decentralized block production, but almost no budget for ecosystem growth.

In general, I am for diversity of nodes. If we really want to fund 3 or even more nodes, someone needs to start coordinating the debate on this topic. We need to be clear about how much we want to invest in decentralization in the long term.

DReps and the community need to understand the consequences of this decision.

Nobody is forcing us to start funding 3 alternative nodes this year. We can start with one node. See if SPOs are interested in installing it. Next year, we can start funding another node. That seems like a responsible approach to me.

Cardano governance lacks coordination and strategic thinking.

Maybe we should fix governance first. Only then can we invest in infrastructure.

author: jasonappleton

text:
Peer review, research rigor & high tech standards are widely regarded as core strengths of the Cardano ecosystem.

But when it comes to evaluating 100s of treasury budget proposals, those standards are nowhere to be found.

That contradiction deserves attention.

Why? Check it👇

As a dRep, even with relatively small voting power, I believe it is important to publicly state my position on the current process for evaluating Budget Proposals in the #Cardano ecosystem.

Last year, 194 budget proposals were submitted through Ekklesia. dReps were implicitly expected to review them voluntarily over just a few weeks.

This is a structural problem.

The current model assumes that dReps collectively have the capacity to review hundreds of complex proposals, often accompanied by long documents and attachments, within a short evaluation cycle.

That assumption is simply not realistic.

In practice, this dynamic encourages rushed decisions, shallow reviews, and poor judgment.

Cardano strongly promotes values such as peer review, research rigor, technical excellence, and reliable software systems.

However, there is a clear inconsistency here.

While the ecosystem emphasizes high standards for engineering and protocol design, the mechanisms currently used to decide how treasury funds are allocated rely on a governance process that remains structurally fragile and poorly incentivized.

This deserves far more attention from the community.

It also deserves more attention from proposers themselves, whether they are seeking treasury funding individually or through structures such as Intersect.

If you genuinely value a high-quality process, then the professional review of proposals should matter to you as well.

From a narrow short-term perspective, weaker scrutiny may appear convenient. Less rigor can make approval easier.

But when proposers ignore this problem, minimize it, or leave it entirely to unpaid volunteers to somehow absorb the burden, they are not only affecting their own proposal.

They are helping normalize a weak decision-making layer for the entire ecosystem.

Serious proposers, especially those confident in the quality of their work, should be willing to support stronger review standards, better incentive structures, and a more professional evaluation process.

A weak review layer may benefit some proposals in the short term.

But in the medium and long term, it increases the likelihood of poor funding decisions, including the approval of proposals that should never have been funded from the treasury.

Anyone who cares about Cardano’s long-term sustainability should be able to recognize this.

Several dReps raised similar concerns already in 2025. So far, however, no clear structural solution has emerged.

The introduction of a 1000 ADA proposal fee suggested by @IntersectCBC may reduce spam, but it remains unclear whether this requirement applies only to on-chain submissions or also to Ekklesia drafts.

If the latter remains unrestricted, the evaluation workload could remain close to last year's volume.

Given this reality, I want to be transparent about how I intend to approach this process as a dRep.

If I do not have the necessary domain expertise or the time required to perform a diligent evaluation, my default vote will be ABSTAIN.

Governance review requires time. A proper evaluation of a proposal can easily involve several hours of work, and complex proposals may require significantly more.

Even when using templates, structured frameworks, or AI tools to assist with the workload, human quality assurance remains necessary.

In practice, if the number of proposals approaches last year's scale, I will likely focus on a subset of proposals where I have stronger domain expertise and sufficient time to conduct a responsible review.

Good governance requires diligence.

And diligence requires time, attention, and realistic expectations about human capacity.

If Cardano wants its governance layer to reflect the same standards of rigor that it applies to protocol development, the community must begin taking these structural constraints seriously.

Ignoring them does not strengthen decentralization.

It simply pushes decision-making toward rushed processes and weak evaluation frameworks.

This discussion deserves much more attention than it currently receives.

However, we also need to debate how many nodes we want to fund this year. Can we really afford to fund multiple nodes given our budget? Maybe it’s wiser to ship one node, see if SPOs are interested in installing it, and start supporting another node next year.

We can certainly fund 3 alt nodes this year. But in that case, the community should agree that we really want it. As an individual DRep, I have no chance to decide this for myself.

No one has organized a debate on this topic. Everyone is just quickly submitting proposals to jump over others. This is not a good approach. Someone needs to start coordinating the decision-making process.

author: strikeperps

text:
@invalid_eutxo @planetmaaz @RodrigoPacini DReps should evaluate all proposals in the context of others. Otherwise, there is a risk that the budget will not be left for the last project, which may be the best in the given sector. Or we overinvest in a category at the beginning, so that there is no more left for another more important category later.

The current system is inefficient because it forces DReps to make decisions individually and without context. In this regard, the Interset approach makes sense. It is an effort to coordinate the process. Our governance noticeably lacks it.

Governance needs roughly this:

- Create buckets per category. For example: Decentralization, marketing, ecosystem, infrastructure, etc.
- DReps should agree on what budget each bucket will be allocated.
- Within the budget for the bucket, they should select the best proposals. It is not necessary to reach the limit in a given bucket.

All proposals should go through the same or similar process. It makes no sense to have multiple systems if none can be enforced.

author: USDMOfficial

text:
Funding will not be available until Q3.
Project Catalyst is paused until a new budget process is implemented. The Cardano Builder (CB) DAO technically remains in place, but there have been calls to defund it, and treasury withdrawals are largely being deferred until the new “budget process” is established. The Open Source Committee (OSC) budget was never intended to replace these mechanisms, only to complement them, and it was designed as a pilot.

In the meantime, the ecosystem is facing a convergence of pressures: a crypto winter, a funding winter, and potentially a development winter if more teams are forced to shut down or leave the ecosystem. There are also growing sensitivities around legal boundaries and speech in governance discussions, which adds another layer of uncertainty. The truth is that Cardano is not dead—but continuing through 2026 in the same state of consensus paralysis could eventually push it in that direction.

Competitive salaries do not currently exist within Cardano in the way they do in more mature markets. The ecosystem simply does not yet have the level of supply and demand needed for compensation to be set internally by market dynamics. Instead, the compensation developers request is largely anchored to external markets, which Cardano currently supplements rather than defines.

There have also been calls to rely on RFP processes to compare competing budgets. In practice, however, there may not be enough competing providers for that model to function effectively. The last time a situation like this emerged, the largest engineering supplier signaled they might leave the ecosystem if they could not fund their operations. While controversial, the concern was understandable from both sides: if the largest engineering team cannot sustain itself, critical capacity disappears. At the same time, without a mature vendor marketplace, an RFP process has limited leverage for negotiating pricing. Cardano already employs a large portion of the global Haskell engineering talent pool. Alternative nodes may increase diversity over time, but those efforts require time to mature before businesses can realistically build on them.

Fiscal conservatism alone will not save the ecosystem. Overspending will not save it either. Governance structures that rely on unpaid labor will struggle to sustain themselves, but overpaying governance participants creates its own problems. Integrations with enterprise partners can help, but those integrations cannot rely indefinitely on teams operating at a loss. Innovation is essential, but innovation rarely happens at scale through unfunded passion alone.

Talent is already leaving or finding other ways to sustain their livelihoods. Cardano remains permissionless for builders at the protocol edge, yet much more constrained when it comes to the operational structures that sustain the core ecosystem. Cardano is not dead yet, but it is closer to that outcome than many may want to admit. If diverging viewpoints continue to lock the system into the same stalled state of consensus, the ecosystem risks undermining itself.

In the broader Web3 landscape, Cardano increasingly resembles an isolated island. Multiple initiatives are working to reconnect it, but there is still a noticeable hesitancy from outside builders and institutions. More observers are watching the ecosystem than actively entering it. That reality needs to be acknowledged.

The power to change course still exists. It sits partly with the community and partly with the institutions coordinating the ecosystem. If those two forces can begin serving each other rather than working at cross purposes, Cardano can continue to grow. Success in areas like Midnight, Bitcoin DeFi integrations, stronger treasury mechanisms, sustainable tooling funding paths, guided innovation programs, and part-time but compensated governance structures could all help stabilize the system.

Cardano may be one of the most successful open source passion projects ever built, supported by one of the most loyal communities in Web3. But unless the ecosystem learns to loosen its grip on every lever of control and allow more pragmatic coordination to emerge, it risks turning from something that is into something that was in the historical record of blockchain development.

The takeaway: risk tolerance must increase if the ecosystem wants meaningful outcomes. Otherwise, the "ever sought after" treasury risks becoming little more than a symbolic reserve of Hoskies.

Spend a little more to make the system relevant this year and 2027, even if it means a 400M NCL, you have 1.6 billion, make the other 1.2 billion worth something.

Ideas for turning the ship, of course doing this with the right vetting and responsible processes in place:

1) Fund 1–3 alternative node implementations to increase resilience and engineering diversity.

2) Fund Draper and capitalize a venture hub with the Cardano Foundation to stimulate startup formation.

3) Fund Liquid DeFi initiatives to expand financial utility within the ecosystem.

4) Establish and fund a decentralized Open Source Program Office–style coordination body to close the open source sustainability gap. I gave the framework for it already.

5) Fix and properly fund Project Catalyst so it can operate as an effective innovation pipeline.

6) Approve budget process requests with a 50% threshold aligned to a clearly defined strategy and vision.
7) Fund Intersect’s operational cycle for at least 2-3 years, giving Jack the runway to stabilize and improve the system without the constant risk of defunding.

8) Maximize the integrations already underway across the ecosystem.

9) Ensure the successful launch and adoption of Midnight.

10) Maximize Bitcoin DeFi opportunities and support the teams working to deliver them.

11) Build a real Developer Relations function that coordinates across entities and supports go-to-market adoption.

12) Invest seriously in marketing. Influencers and external voices have already drifted away—bring them back and expand the reach. Cardano needs to be present in the places where industry narratives are formed.
author: DeOpenSourceGuy

text:
Cardano just launched programmable compliance tokens. Here's how it stacks up against Ethereum's ERC-3643 👇

The @Cardano_CF has released CIP-0113, which allows token issuers to embed enforceable compliance logic directly to native assets on the network.

CIP-0113 and ERC-3643 are both token standards designed for programmable, compliant tokens representing real-world assets (RWAs), but they operate on different blockchains with distinct architectural approaches.

🔸 Core Platforms
CIP-0113 is a Cardano Improvement Proposal for programmable tokens on the Cardano blockchain, enabling smart contract-driven logic for token behaviors like compliance checks using Plutus V2 scripts.

ERC-3643 is an Ethereum standard for permissioned tokens on EVM-compatible chains, achieving Final status in December 2023 after originating from Tokeny's protocol in 2017. It builds on ERC-20 with added compliance layers.

🔸 Compliance Mechanisms
ERC-3643 enforces regulatory compliance through a multi-contract suite: ONCHAINID for decentralized identities (using ERC-734/735), Identity Registry, Trusted Issuers Registry, Claim Topics Registry, and a Compliance contract that validates transfers on-chain without exposing sensitive data.

CIP-0113 achieves similar programmable compliance via Cardano's native script-based tokens, emulating account-model behaviors on UTXO with features like investor whitelisting and transfer restrictions, but relies on Plutus for custom logic rather than modular registries.

🔸 Adoption and Scope
ERC-3643 has tokenized ~$28B in RWAs, with 100+ association members (e.g., Chainlink, DTCC) and engagements in 21+ regulators, including SEC mentions.

CIP-0113 remains a proposal, focused on Cardano interoperability for programmable tokens but with less mature adoption data.

🔸 Final thoughts
It's exciting to see more and more standards and mechanisms going to market to solve the structural issues of compliant asset tokenization. We are still in relatively early stages in the innovation process. Besides that,  different asset classes and institutions might require a different approach.

(The fact that I like to drive a Volvo but my friend likes to drive a BMW is okay. As long as we can both reach our destination, in compliance with all the traffic rules, keeping us and others safe.)

I look forward to seeing more institutional activity on other chains as well and congrats to the Cardano team!

What do you think? Will there be one final standard ruling asset tokenization?

text:
@EPOCHpool Directors are roughly 25k each and we have 2. Supervisors are about 10k, and we have 1. Committee members can be variable and paid 1k ADA/month.

In general, yearly costs will be under 100k for non-committee members. This will be a nonprofit, so no taxes.

While these costs seem high, we did look into other jurisdictions such st the US. The issue is that we can get nonprofit legal status at the state level, but not the federal level. Federal taxes are 21%.

If we hit our target of 4% returns on 50million ADA (~12.5million USD), thats about 500k in revenue with about 100k paid in taxes not including ancillary costs for filing taxes.

So the current structure in the worst case scenario is break even assuming we only take out 50 million ada. The costs for the legal entity remain static regardless of scale, which is one reason I floated the idea of 100 million ada instead of 50.

text:
Cardano Daily - 2026-03-09

🏗️ 生态发展
Cardano 基金会正式推出 CIP-0113，引入大规模可编程代币标准。该标准使代币发行方能够直接在 Cardano 原生资产上执行合规逻辑，采用模块化开源框架，目前已在 Preview 测试网上线。社区反响热烈，开发者已开始构建 JavaScript/TypeScript 实现，并计划举办专门的开发者答疑会帮助建设者上手。

在重要的技术里程碑方面，Michael Fazio 发布了基于 Rust 的 Cardano 节点实现,将开发者生态从 Haskell 扩展到更广泛的领域。社区成员指出,这可以吸引更多 Rust 开发者围绕节点基础设施构建创新用例。

集成进展方面,Fireblocks 正在审计 Cardano 支持代码,Archax(英国 FCA 监管的数字资产交易所)已将 Cardano 集成到其机构代币化引擎中。Pyth Network 的一级预言机集成也得到确认,进一步强化了 Cardano 的 DeFi 基础设施。

⚖️ 治理讨论
社区正在就国库管理和资金优先级的根本问题展开激烈讨论。核心争议之一是 DRep 是否应该有权调整提案中的请求金额,而不是只能批准或拒绝。有社区成员指出,在大多数政府资金系统中,决策者通常会根据自己的评估批准少于请求的金额——而这一机制目前在 Cardano 的链上国库中缺失。

Cardano 基金会对"Epochs 613-713 期间 3 亿 ADA 净变化限额"治理行动投了赞成票,表示该限额与 2025 年国库流入相符。然而,多位社区成员质疑在一年内急于资助三个不同的节点多样性提案,认为在现阶段用户获取和链上流动性应该优先于基础设施冗余。

随着 Catalyst F15/F16 被取消,一些人认为这是一个机会,可以用更有针对性、更专业的方法来填补资金缺口,而不是继续当前模式。

💬 社区声音
围绕优先级的讨论成为反复出现的主题:社区正在辩论 Cardano 应该专注于技术基础设施还是用户获取。多位声音认为,虽然节点多样性和替代实现有价值,但生态系统的当务之急是采用、流动性和链上交易。

一种观点认为,Cardano 正在"追赶更发达的链上经济体",应该专注于资助能够推动采用的专业团队,而不是在没有明确财富来源的情况下创建主权财富基金等官僚结构。

关于治理有效性的辩论也浮出水面,一些人认为当前系统"制造的障碍多于创造的价值",建议基金会应该更直接地被激励去将资金用于生态系统改进。

🌐 行业观察
比特币达到里程碑,总供应量的 95% 已被挖出(2100 万中的 2000 万 BTC),但市场情绪仍然充满挑战。一位分析师指出,BTC 本周未能通过两次身份测试:在疲弱的 NFP 数据(降息叙事)和伊朗冲突升级(数字黄金叙事)时都没有上涨,反而跟随风险资产下跌。

币安创始人 CZ 预测"AI 代理将进行比人类多 100 万倍的支付,并将使用加密货币",Cardano 的 x402 标准已经与 Coinbase 对齐,为专注于代理支付的 ADA 建设者提供支持。埃隆·马斯克确认 X Money 将支持转账、存款和借贷,如果为 6 亿用户集成比特币访问,可能会加速加密货币的采用。

更广泛的加密市场表现参差不齐,DeXe(+19.86%)、Bittensor(+11.49%)和 Chilliz(+10.55%)领涨 24 小时涨幅榜,而主要资产仍承压。

text:
Be prepared for me to post about this often.

Id like to make this happen as quickly as possible.

But, I also want to be considerate of your time.

Right now is a good time to vote on this because there are two other proposals that are live.

The first is from @blinklabs_io for Dingo, the Go node that they have been developing without compensation for years. Its currently useful as a data node, but they want to finish building it to be a block producing node. This would help with node diversity.

In addition to this, there is the Amaru proposal, which I think everyone is familiar with. They successfully completed their tasks last year, and as good stewards of treasury funds, returned what was left over after completing their milestones. They submitted a new proposal with the next set of milestones for development. Amaru is a Rust node, and Ive physically observed it run on a raspberry pi (although sadly I did not get one 😢). It is super efficient, but more importantly it helps to improve node diversity in our ecosystem.

text:
Houston, we have achieved common sense escape velocity!

🔹 I voted Yes on this Intersect info action because it raises the quality of treasury review before capital ever leaves the pad.

🔹 The point is straightforward. Better governance starts with better inputs. If DReps and the community are going to make sound onchain decisions, proposals need to be structured for real comparison and real due diligence.

🔹 This info action helps move us away from persuasive writing alone and toward reviewable standards that let everyone compare proposals on equal footing.

🔹 The strongest parts for me are clear:

🔹 Strategic Alignment and KPI Targeting
Proposers should show how their request connects to a real framework and what outcomes they expect to deliver.
🔹 Work Package Based Budgeting
Budgets should be tied to defined work, expected value, measurable outputs, and clear cost categories.

🔹 Identity and Commitment Verification
Proposers should show who they are, what entity stands behind the request, and proof of commitment to the process.

🔹 If treasury resources are meant to serve ecosystem wide public benefit, then this kind of proposal discipline is not friction. It is escape velocity for better decisions. @IntersectMBO @IntersectCPC @IntersectCIVICS @Intersect_GMC @IntersectTSC @Intersect_MCC

author: MinswapIntern

text:
We voted.

Governance Action:

📌 Intersect Budget Process Framework (2026)

🗳️ Vote: 🔴 NO

Coordination is needed, but the framework still leaves key structural risks unresolved: bundling ambiguity, unrealistic review assumptions & unclear NCL usage.

Full Review below 👇

What is being voted on?

➡️ An Info Action to formalize the Intersect Budget Process Framework for 2026 and future cycles. It does not approve spending directly, but it may become the reference process for how Treasury-funded initiatives are structured, reviewed, prioritized, consolidated into Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions, and later executed.

More info on the Governance Action below 👇
🔗https://t.co/KgXZ0rr13k

What did @AgoraCardano evaluate?

🧾 The full framework text and the institutional assumptions behind its budgeting workflow

📉 How proposals are structured and compared (strategic pillars, KPIs, work packages)

🧠 Structural governance risks: bundling dynamics, review overload, and coordination assumptions

⏱️ Review capacity vs process design, including the reliance on “Deep Review” by DReps

📌 The relationship between the framework, Net Change Limit usage, and real budget demand

🔹 Whether the process meaningfully addresses coordination gaps observed in prior budget cycles

🔹 Whether the framework resolves known governance risks or simply formalizes them

Why did we vote 🔴 NO?

🔼 Upsides

✅ A coordination layer for ecosystem budgeting is necessary. Treasury funding cannot rely solely on uncoordinated submissions while expecting the community to infer priorities in real time.

✅ Aligning proposals with Cardano Vision 2030 pillars and KPIs improves strategic comparability.

✅ Work-package budgeting improves transparency and makes proposal structures easier to evaluate.

🔽 Downsides

⚠️ Bundling risk remains unresolved. The framework does not clearly restrict how work packages may aggregate distinct initiatives, enabling potential “all-or-nothing” proposal structures.

⚠️ Heavy reliance on ���deep review” by DReps is unrealistic given current workload and incentive conditions.

⚠️ Review capacity constraints are acknowledged in practice but not addressed structurally in the framework.

⚠️ NCL usage and contingency posture remain underspecified. The framework implies that the annual cycle may consume available fiscal space without defining reserve or emergency mechanisms.

⚠️ The spending ceiling (NCL) is effectively determined before a structured view of real budget demand is produced.

⚠️ Consultation is referenced but lacks a transparent public trail documenting how feedback shaped the framework.

Coordination itself is not the problem.

The issue is institutional robustness.

If this framework becomes the reference process for Treasury allocation, then bundling safeguards, realistic review assumptions, and clearer fiscal governance posture cannot remain ambiguous.
Roughly one year has already passed since the 2025 budget cycle.

If these structural issues are not addressed, they should at least be openly discussed and explained, including why they are not being incorporated into the framework.

Want to dig deeper?

📄 I published a detailed review of the proposed Intersect ecosystem budgeting framework for Cardano. Check it out 👇🏻

🔗 https://t.co/VBwTS0r2Vu

📄 Full  voting rationale 👇🏻

🔗https://t.co/5NDow5Hg9i

If independent, structured governance reviews resonate with you, delegate to @AgoraCardano.

Full guide 👇🏼

author: P2PValidator

text:
For custodians, funds, and exchanges evaluating staking infrastructure, slashing risk becomes a governance and operational question.

Architecture matters.

Change management matters.

Client diversity matters.
author: P2PValidator

text:
Correlated slashing may occur when:

1. infrastructure environments are identical
2. validator clients are uniform
3. signing systems are centralized
4. failover logic is duplicated
author: P2PValidator

text:
Ethereum slashing is protocol-enforced and irreversible.

But the bigger institutional concern isn’t isolated slashing.

It’s correlated ethereum slashing across multiple validators.
author: P2PValidator

text:
Many institutions entering ETH staking ask:

“What protocol reward rate can we expect?”

But the better question is:

“How do we evaluate ethereum slashing risk across validator infrastructure?”

text:
Yoroi W₳llet DRep votes "YES" on the governance action documenting the Cardano Budget Process Framework for the 2026 budget cycle. 🗳️

The framework by @IntersectMBO introduces a clearer process for how ecosystem treasury budgets are proposed, reviewed, and executed. It defines standardized proposal requirements, KPIs, and work package budgeting while outlining milestone based disbursements and oversight mechanisms.

This helps DReps and the community evaluate proposals more consistently and strengthens accountability in the use of treasury funds. Yoroi supports documenting this framework to provide clearer structure and transparency for the ecosystem budgeting process.

text:
🚨💡🌐TypeScript「ブラウザで動くノード」とPebble「新スマコン言語」──HLabsの2026年Cardano開発計画

HLabs（Harmonic Laboratories）が、2026年に向けたCardanoトレジャリー予算提案のドラフトを公開しました。

正式提出は2〜3週間後を予定しており、現在コミュニティからのフィードバックを募集しています。
今回の提案は、Cardanoエコシステムの「開発基盤」を強化するかなり重要な内容です。

ポイントを整理すると、主に3つの柱で構成されています。

■ 1. Gerolamo：TypeScript製のCardanoノード

HLabsが開発している Gerolamo は、TypeScriptで書かれたCardanoノードです。

特徴は「ブラウザでも動く可能性があるノード」という点。

これにより次のようなメリットが出てきます。
・SPOが 追加のリレーノードとして使える
・dAppが 中央集権APIに依存せずチェーンデータへ直接アクセスできる
・ライトウォレットが Daedalus級の検証能力を持てる
特に重要なのは クライアント多様性です。

現在Cardanoは基本的にHaskellノードが中心ですが、
別の実装が増えることで
・単一実装バグのリスク低減
・ネットワーク耐障害性向上
といった、ブロックチェーンとして非常に重要な強化になります。

Gerolamoはまず リレー用途をターゲットにしており、
将来的にはブロック生成ノードになる可能性も示唆されています。

■ 2. Pebble：新しいスマートコントラクト言語

もう一つの柱が Pebble というスマートコントラクト言語。

Cardanoのスマコンはこれまで
・Haskell
・Plutus
・Aiken
など 関数型プログラミング寄りの言語が多く、
Web2開発者にはややハードルが高いという課題がありました。

Pebbleはここを解決するために
・TypeScript / JavaScriptに近い文法
・命令型スタイル
・それでも最終的にはUPLCにコンパイル
という設計になっています。

つまり
Web2 / Solidity / JS開発者をCardanoへ呼び込むオンランプという位置づけです。

HLabsの説明では、
世界には 1700万人以上のJS/TS開発者 がいるため、

Cardanoの開発者プールを大きく拡張できる可能性があります。

なおPebbleは Aikenと競合ではなく補完関係と説明されています。

■ 3. TypeScriptツールチェーンの維持

HLabsは実は多くのCardano開発ツールをメンテしています。

例えば
・cardano-ledger-ts
・ouroboros-miniprotocols-ts
・uplc
・plutus-machine
など。

これらは多くのプロジェクトが依存している基盤ライブラリです。

ハードフォークが起きた際、
これらが更新されないと
・dAppが壊れる
・ライブラリ分裂
・セキュリティ問題
といったリスクが出ます。

今回の提案では
ハードフォーク対応���ンテナンス
も正式な予算項目として含まれています。

■ 予算規模

提案予算は
約803万ADA（約225万ドル＋25%コンティンジェンシー）

内訳は
・Gerolamo開発
・Pebble開発
・ハードフォーク対応
合計 10FTE規模の開発体制。

未使用資金はトレジャリーへ返却される仕組みで、

エスクローはスマートコントラクトで管理されます。
さらに
・TxPipe
・BlinkLabs
・Aiken
関係者による 独立監督ボード が資金管理を監視します。

■ Cardano2030戦略との整合

この提案はCardanoの2030戦略とも直接リンクしています。
特に重要なのは
「複数ノード実装」
というKPIです。

Gerolamoは

Cardanoノード
Amaru
BlinkLabs

などと並ぶ

新しいクライアント実装
として位置づけられています。

■ 個人的に面白いポイント

今回の提案は、
派手なアプリではなく 基盤インフラ投資です。

しかし実際には
・ノード多様化
・開発者オンボーディング
・ツール維持
という
ブロックチェーンの長期的な成長に直結する領域です。

特に
「ブラウザで動くノード」
というコンセプトは、
dAppアーキテクチャを大きく変える可能性があります。

もし実現すれば、
「dAppが自分でチェーンを検証する」
という本来の分散型モデルにかなり近づきます。

コミュニティフィードバックを受けて、この提案は今後数週間で正式なトレジャリー提案として提出される予定です。

SPO、DRep、開発者にとっては、
かなり重要な議論になりそうです。

text:
The biggest threats to our ecosystem succeeding from within @IntersectMBO are:
- @radioastro (Budget Committee Lead)
- @NicolasC3rny (Civics Committee Lead and Budget Committee Seat) 

I can confidently state this with my years of experience with Intersect. 

Although both are nice guys, they are not working in best interest of the community and have failed so massively already in their roles. 

Let’s start with Lloyd; his background with DripDropz, the early entry into Intersect, he was fundamental in designing Intersect as well as establishing the first round of CDH Grants from Intersect that went out 2024-25. These same grants he oversaw he also applied for; and granted himself. 

We gave Lloyd money for a few things, events, marketing, and content creation beyond his day to day paycheck from his work as a committee lead. These proposals included the GM Cardano YouTube channel; we paid 106,000 $ADA for him to have less than 5,000 views across the entire lifetime of the channel. Here is the channel an the grant:
GM Cardano
https://t.co/UcB6spqMqD
Lloyds CDH Grant for 106,000 $ADA
https://t.co/S0ETJLHPB7

If we can’t trust Lloyd to not take advantage of and drain our treasury from the inside on pointless garbage like this; we cannot allow him to design our budget processes. 

Now on to Nico; I’ve long wondered what’s the deal with this guy. Nice enough. But he has consistently failed at his duties to the Cardano community in his role as Civics Committee Lead. 

Per @IOHK_Charles from my last discussion with him a few weeks ago; it was Intersect Civics that was handed the task of delivering the ‘Future Workstreams’ for the Cardano Constitution. This included vital areas like the Definitions WG, Amendments Process, and additional scope for the document. 

Nico has failed so miserably at this and ignored the large subset of delegates that have pursued answers on this matter. 

Another threat from Nico to our ecosystem is his consistent push for higher spending without any regard or consideration for the waste we have ongoing. Being seated on the Budget Committee gives his stance weight we cannot currently balance against. 

I bring these matter to the ecosystem at large since I alone cannot challenge these men and their decisions.

text:
I keep seeing people say “users are leaving Cardano” or “there are no users.”

That completely misunderstands how adoption works.

The real issue isn’t that users left.

It’s that mainstream users haven’t arrived yet in meaningful numbers.

Most people don’t care about blockchain.
They don’t care about consensus models or tokenomics.

They care about utility.

Users don’t appear because we built infrastructure.

They appear when someone builds something so useful people use it without caring what’s under the hood.

Just like capital has to flow into an economy, users have to flow into an ecosystem.

And those users come from the mainstream world, not crypto Twitter.

They arrive when we build applications normal people actually want to use.

text:
@AlmaMater07 Unlike ETH, Solana, and others, Cardano has a fixed supply just like Bitcoin. So I actually wouldn’t be in favor of burning the supply for a layer 1 with such tokenomics because once all the supply is in circulation, there will be no way to fund innovative products we need to scale.

Instead, I would decrease the yearly budget (currently 350m Ada) immediately since we have many years of proof that a very high number of that goes to waste, and change the focus on what should be receiving funding.

Currently it is mostly infrastructure projects being funded, every year over and over infra infra infra, building the next thing that Cardano “needs”, with no sense of focus towards retail, business, or product adoption. We just build improved infrastructure non-stop which to me is a massive mistake. It has become a playground for dev builds regardless of the number of users here. 

I would:
👉🏼 Lower the NCL immediately 
👉🏼 Fund the top 10 products on Cardano that show a plan to scale and bring users from outside the ecosystem, with a strong focus towards perps, prediction markets, and token launchpads
👉🏼 Incentivize a yield boost to stables on Cardano using subsidies, that way users see real benefits to minting USDCx and deploying it in our ecosystem - this wouldn’t cost much to beat out the 3-4% yield offered in other chains and wouldn’t cost us a ton 
👉🏼 Fund Cardano related influencers to share content on recent product advancements and growth 

Right now perps, prediction markets, and token launchpads are dominating crypto volume on all chains except for Cardano. These are products that bring real volume which in turn leads to adoption and more fees to the treasury. When these products thrive, the entire ecosystem of other products including dex’s and lending protocols grows alongside it. I would put a strong emphasis on scaling those niches as well as providing immediate yield boosts on USDCx, while simultaneously scaling back on endless infrastructure dev. 

This is a far superior strategy than anything we’ve done as a chain for so many years now.

I’ve been off X for a week, and unfortunately I’ve missed some opportunities to comment on the current budget situation here in #Cardano. 

Here are some names, some facts and some things to keep in mind in the coming weeks of Cardano Budget and Elections Chaos. 

- The current Budget Committee and many of the rest of Intersect Committee seat holders are the same people that spent our money into this situation. Some names you should all be familiar with:
Adam Rusch (Summon, ADAO; also was sole application for several 2024 Intersect grants in the 300,000 and up range, Adam here is a Board Member of Intersect)

Lloyd Duhon (GM Cardano, Drip Dropz; also milked the 2024 Intersect grants in the hundreds of thousands for GM Cardano YouTube Channel, this guy is in charge of Intersect Budget Committee)

Kyle Solomon (DripDropz; Product Committee Seat Holder and catalyst graduate, thinks he’s business development extraordinaire, all subsidized) 

Nicholas Cerny (Cardano Foundation; Civics Committee Lead, and Budget Committee member. A major reason Constitution Future Workstreams stalled out, and a major proponent of high spending at these times)

Ian McCullah (Intersect North American Hub, AWEN; Intersect Members and Community Committee member. Ian is the former manager of the Intersect NA hub and why that program failed in North America. He also is a career grant writer and lobbyist in real life with several catalyst rounds and other funding thru Intersect) 

Kriss Baird (Catalyst; current seat holder on the Budget Committee in Intersect. Proponent of and major contributor to wasted funding over the last 3 years) 

James Meidinger (USDM, Moneta; currently on Product Committee, cabal member, gatekeeper extraordinaire, and major proponent of increased spending at these times) 

 I am a firm believer that if we can effectively remove and replace these individuals we will save the ecosystem money and remove some of the power held by the current ‘Higher Spending’ crowd here in governance. If you don’t believe me, just look their names up in catalyst and Intersect knowledge base. See how much these people have as specific persons took from the ecosystem. Look at their decisions in committees. We cannot trust people like Lloyd to make budget decisions; he had us pay 106,000 $ADA for a YouTube channel with less than 5,000 impressions. We cannot trust people like Cullah to make decisions on MCC Hubs when he is one of the primary drivers behind Hubs failures in 2024 and 25. 

- Most of these funded teams have BEEN overestimating their own hourly and annual wages/value. $225,000 a year, times 4 people and that’s only one proposal. There are so many more like this. The jelly cubes guy is another good example. These Cardano people the grifters that have reached moderate success and gained leverage and runway via catalyst are WORSE at managing this money. They spend so fast and do not reason with the market conditions or their revenue streams. Cut them all off. Let them wither away. Build sustainability from the start or don’t build at all. 

- Charles, his loyal followers, and the cabal of beggars around him were always the biggest threat to our ecosystem; no matter how much he tries to blame us as the community for his failings. Remember, he didn’t care about our input over the years. Do not accept blame or responsibility for him and his poorly managed cabal.

author: ADAFrog_Pool

text:
@ADAFrog_Pool @phil_uplc It's just a smart contract standard: you put the payment credential in the *staking key*, and the programmable logic in the *payment key*; so the tokens are *always* held by the payment credential, but can identify who "owns" the token by the staking credential.

So it:
1) tells developers how to build such tokens
2) maintains a central on-chain registry so that such tokens are discoverable
3) tells wallets how to find tokens belonging to the user and spend them
4) tells defi how to integrate them

@phil_uplc lemme know if that summary is inaccurate in any way. ofc I'm simplifying a lot

author: Quantumplation

text:
Thank you to @phil_uplc in particular! I know he's been working very hard on this standard for a very long time, and drove a *ton* of the early conversation, such as around the Wyoming stablecoin initiative. We've chatted many times about the designs, and he's helped me plan out what the integration would look like for SundaeSwap contracts in the future. His one line mention in the blog post doesn't really do his effort justice, in my opinion. 😅

I'm also a bit confused by the last minute switch to Aiken, and I have lower confidence in those than the original Plutarch contracts; I could understand if the examples were in Aiken, but the core validators, for the registry etc. don't need to be "developer accessible"; they need to be performant and secure, since they won't be updated on an ongoing basis.

That's not to say the Aiken contracts *aren't* performant or secure, but it's just something I'll have to spend some time evaluating first.

Overall, though, great to see the increase in options for token standards across the ecosystem!

text:
While there are always reasons behind how things play out, I’ll say the unpopular part out loud: if you want the Cadillac, you have to pay for it.

And it's not my problem to solve anymore but 2 cents to consider:

Negotiation and cost justification matter, but you can’t bargain down the value of experience and quality when that’s exactly what the role demands. Sure, there are cheaper alternatives, but when compensation doesn’t reflect the level of expertise required, the people capable of delivering that quality will simply take their skills elsewhere.

Businesses understand this well. If you want someone truly dedicated to a project, you also have to ensure their livelihood is secure. You can see the same dynamic anywhere in the real world—from the reception desk at a luxury hotel to the front desk of a highway motel. The basic tasks might look similar on paper, but the level of service, professionalism, and reliability comes from investing in the people who deliver it.

At the end of the day, you’re choosing which room you want to stay in. And if you’re building a financial network that carries billions in value, you build skyscrapers and pay the engineers who know how to construct them, you don’t hire someone to build a doghouse where that kind of liability is associated.

That being said, due diligence still matters. Make sure the team you’re investing in actually matches the experience reflected in their résumé. At the same time, respect reasonable privacy boundaries—there are legal considerations around what information can and should be shared. The goal is to balance proper vetting with professional respect.

text: @amw7 Attackers cannot save up dust. Dust is like voltage, it’s generated up to some capacity as determined by the source (in this case the amount of Night tokens you hold), and either you use it or you lose it, you won’t be able to just indefinitely stack it by not spending it, you’ll generate X amount of dust periodically based on your night holdings and it decays at a rate such that at any given time the most dust you will ever be able to have is X. If you don’t use any for three years, you’ll still only have X Dust.

text:
🚨💡🌐Cardanoの新標準CIP-0113とは？規制対応トークンの基盤が登場

Cardanoにとってかなり重要なアップデートが公開されました。それが CIP-0113「Programmable Tokens」 です。

簡単に言うと、Cardanoのネイティブトークンに“ルール（コンプライアンスロジック）”を直接組み込める標準が登場しました。

現在はPreviewテストネットで公開されており、誰でも試すことができます。

なぜこれが重要なのか

Cardanoのネイティブアセットモデルはとても強力です。

・ADAと同じレベルで扱われる
・すべてのウォレットやエクスプローラーで表示できる
・同じインフラで転送できる
つまり
非常にシンプルでスケーラブルな設計です。

ただし一つ課題がありました。

一度トークンを発行すると、発行者がそのトークンの振る舞いを制御する仕組みがないという点です。

例えば
・KYCユーザーのみ送金可能
・制裁リストのアドレスを拒否
・法的命令による凍結
・特定地域のみ利用可能
といった規制対応ロジックをネイティブトークンに組み込めなかったのです。

CIP-0113はこの問題を解決します。
この標準によりトークン自体にルールを組み込むことが可能になります。

例えば
・mint条件
・burn条件
・transfer条件
などをスマートコントラクトで定義し、
トークンが動くたびにネットワークが自動検証する仕組みです。

例：
「送信者が制裁リストにいないかチェック」
「認証済みウォレット同士のみ送金可能」
といった処理が
オンチェーンで自動実行されます。

この仕組みが重要なのは、現実世界資産（RWA）トークン化の前提条件だからです。

財団の記事では以下のトークン化用途が想定されています。

金融資産
・株式
・債券
・証券
・デリバティブ
・ファンド

通貨
・ステーブルコイン
・CBDC

実物資産
・不動産
・アート
・ジュエリー
・コモディティ

無形資産
・知的財産
・カーボンクレジット
・再生エネルギークレジット

これらはすべて
KYC / AML / 規制対応
が必要です。

つまり
「ルール付きトークン」
がないと実用化できません。

CIP-0113はCardanoにこの共通フレームワークを導入するものです。

CIP-0113はモジュール型アーキテクチャになっています。
コア標準は「プログラマブルトークンの枠組み」だけを定義します。

実際のルールは
サブスタンダード
として追加できます。

つまり

・企業
・DeFi
・規制金融
・RWA

それぞれが
独自ルールをテンプレートとして追加可能です。

この設計によって
将来の規制変更にも対応可能
という非常に柔軟な構造になっています。

エコシステムにとっての意味

これまでCardanoでは
プロジェクトごとに
独自のコンプライアンスロジックを実装していました。

その結果
・ウォレット
・エクスプローラー
・DApps
がトークンごとに個別対応する必要がありました。

これは
・開発コスト増加
・スケール阻害
・機関投資家の参入障壁
になっていました。

CIP-0113はこの共通標準を作ることでエコシステム全体のスケールを可能にします。

現在の状況は、

・Previewテストネット公開
・オープンソース
・Aiken実装
・開発者フィードバック募集

という段階です。

今後は

・セキュリティ監査
・プラットフォーム拡張
・証券トークン用サブスタンダード
などが予定されています。

個人的に重要だと思うポイントとしては、

Cardanoは以前から
ネイティブアセットモデル
という強い基盤を持っていました。

そこに今回
プログラマブルなコンプライアンスレイヤー
が追加されました。

これは
・ステーブルコイン
・RWA
・機関金融
をCardanoに持ち込む上でかなり重要な基盤になります。

トークン化市場は
世界で数十兆ドル規模になると言われています。

今回のCIP-0113は
Cardanoがその市場を取りに行くためのインフラ整備
と言えるでしょう。

3月19日には開発者向けのデモセッションも予定されています。

Cardanoのトークン化戦略がここからどう広がるのか、かなり注目です。

text:
🚨💡🌐IOGがUSDCxブリッジ手数料を90日延長──USDCxブリッジ“手数料0ドル”

IOGから興味深い発表がありました。USDC → Cardano のブリッジ手数料（USDC → USDCx）が引き続き0ドルのままになります。しかも今回、この無料期間がさらに90日延長されました。

USDCxの勢いが伸びていることを受けて、Cardanoへ資金を移しやすい状態をしばらく維持する、という判断のようです。

USDCxは、
USDCを裏付けにしてCardano上で利用できるドルステーブルコインです。

簡単に言うと、
・他チェーンのUSDCを預ける
・Cardano上でUSDCxが発行される
・逆に戻すときはUSDCxをバーンしてUSDCを引き出す
という仕組みです。

つまり、CardanoのDeFiでUSDC流動性を使えるようにするための基盤と言えます。

今回の「手数料0ドル延長」が意味するものは、単なるキャンペーンではありません。IOGは明確にCardanoにドル流動性を呼び込むフェーズに入っています。

ブリッジのコストがあると、
・トレーダー
・DeFiユーザー
・流動性提供者
が移動しにくくなります。

そこで最初の段階では移動コストをゼロにして流動性を集めるという戦略ですね。

これはDeFiではよく使われる「流動性ブートストラップ」の方法です。

Cardanoはこれまで
・ネイティブステーブルコイン
・アルゴリズム型
・地域型
などはいくつかありましたが、
世界最大級のドル流動性（USDC）を直接取り込む動き
は、かなり大きな変化です。

もしUSDC流動性が本格的に流れ込むと、
・DEX流動性
・レンディング
・DeFi市場
が一気に活性化する可能性があります。

USDCxがどこまで広がるか。

ここは今年のCardanoエコシステムを見る上で、かなり重要なポイントになりそうですね。

text:
🚨💡🌐Cardanoの単一実装リスクに挑むDingo財務提案

Cardanoでかなり重要な提案が出ています。
Blink Labsの Dingo treasury proposal が現在ライブで、GovTool上では財務引き出し提案として提出済みです。提出日は 2026年3月6日、期限は 2026年3月25日 と表示されています。

これは、Go言語で開発されているCardanoノード実装 Dingo を、12か月でメインネットのブロック生成対応まで仕上げるための資金提案 です。

提案額は 6,900,000 ADA（約207万ドル相当）。

SPOやDRep、ビルダーにレビューを呼びかける形で公開されています。

まず、ここで理解しておきたいのは、この提案は「新しいノードをゼロから作る」という話ではありません。

Dingoはすでにかなり開発が進んでいて、

・コミット数 1,226
・追加コード 約20万行
・リリース 48回
・ジェネシスからの同期可能
・Mithrilスナップショットから起動可能
・314 / 314 の適合テスト合格
・Plutus V1 / V2 / V3対応（V4開発中）

という状態まで来ています。

つまり、すでに「実験段階」はかなり越えていて、インフラ用途のノードバックエンドとしては実用レベルに近いところまで来ています。

ただし、まだ一つ決定的に足りないものがあります。

それがメインネットでのブロック生成能力（Block Producer）です。

今回の提案は、そこを完成させることを目的にしています。

具体的にはこの12か月で
・メインネットブロック生成対応
・Dijkstraハードフォーク対応
・Leios実装準備
・外部セキュリティ監査
・本番運用の堅牢化
・オペレーター向けドキュメント整備
まで持っていく、というロードマップです。

そして、この提案の背景には、Cardanoがずっと抱えている構造的なテーマがあります。

それがノード多様性（node diversity）です。

現在、Cardanoのブロック生成は基本的に Haskellノード実装 に依存しています。
これは設計として非常に強力ではありますが、ブロックチェーンの世界では一般的に単一クライアント依存はリスクと考えられています。

例えばEthereumは、
・Geth
・Nethermind
・Besu
・Erigon
など複数のクライアントがあり、1つの実装に問題が出てもネットワーク全体は止まりません。

Cardanoでも、最近はこの方向へ動き始めています。

代表的なのが Amaru です。

AmaruはRust製のCardanoノードで、PragmaやCardano Foundationなどが関わっています。

つまり、構造としては

Haskellノード
＋
Rustノード（Amaru）
＋
Goノード（Dingo）

という形でマルチクライアント構造を作ろうとしている流れです。

Dingo提案も、この文脈の中にあります。

特にBlink Labsが強調しているのはGoという言語のエコシステムです。

Goは、
・Ethereum（Geth）
・Cosmos
・Avalanche
など多くのチェーンのインフラで使われている言語です。

さらに
・Coinbase
・Binance
・Kraken
などの取引所インフラでもバックエンドはGoがかなり多い。

つまり、Goノードがあると、インフラ企業や開発者がCardanoに入りやすくなるという考え方です。

一方で、この提案には慎重な意見もあります。

主に議論になっているのは
・6.9M ADAという予算規模
・Amaruとの役割の重複
・12か月で本当にメインネット対応できるのか
このあたりです。

ただ、提案側もガバナンス設計はかなり意識していて、
・マイルストーンごとの支払い
・独立オーバーサイトボード
・公開トランザクションジャーナル
・月次／四半期レポート
・未使用資金のトレジャリー返還
といった仕組みも入っています。

なので今回の議論は
Cardanoはマルチクライアント戦略をどこまで本気で進めるのかという、かなり本質的なテーマだと思います。

将来、
・Leios
・Dijkstra
・さらなるスケー���ング
などが進んでいく中で、実装の多様性をどこまで持つべきかこれはSPOやDRepにとっても、かなり重要な判断材料になるテーマです。

興味がある方は、ぜひ提案本文も読んでみてください。

コミュニティとして、こういう議論が増えているのはとても健全だと思います。

提案内容
👇
https://t.co/28V5dZaWyf

author: CashAnvil

text:
@amw7 1. Anybody holding NIGHT is allowed to spam useless transactions that cost them nothing other than time. 

Right now blockchain in general has an inverse incentive problem:

Everyone wants their favorite chain to have lots of users, activity and other relevant KPIs, people want to use blockchain for things that don’t strictly relate to DeFi (gaming, supply chain, immutable record keeping, music royalties, and so on), but those same people don’t want to contribute to those KPIs because for the average user L1 tokens are the main investment vehicle, they buy ETH because they want it to go up in value so they can sell it, those users would certainly be interested in actually using the network but they would have to pay (and thus lose money) and that would defeat the whole purpose of why they entered in the first place (to make money). By separating the investment vehicle (L1 token) from the gas currency, you fix that inverse incentive problem, such that there is no reason not to actively use the chain for any useful purpose (as opposed to using the chain strictly for DeFi or other money making activity). So users are now economically incentivized to actually use the chain, they want the network to have lots of daily active users, transactions and other KPIs, and it doesn’t cost them any money to actively contribute to those KPIs (instead it increases the visibility and public perception of their investment, the L1 token, which can benefit them financially). 

Without separating the investment vehicle (L1 token) from the gas currency, every single application must be made through the lense of financial incentives. Developing a web3 game? You can’t just make a good game, you have to make a good game that has a balanced economy and economic incentives and opportunities that counterbalance the network fees required by transactions that must be made to play the game (creating a balanced sustainable in-game economy is immeasurably more difficult than simply making an enjoyable game). 

As for DoS mitigations, to transact you will need to hold dust, the dust generation formula is dynamic with respect to Midnight’s bandwidth, it would require significant Night to generate enough dust to perform successful liveness attacks, and because the network is federated at launch even if a malicious actor had enough Night and the desire to use its dust to DoS the network it still could be prevented with spam filtering by the federated nodes.

author: CashAnvil

text:
@goofy_crisp @bigpey @DjediMindTrick I'm sorry that's how you feel about it man, but you literally haven't spent a minute in there. You know i love you but the things you are saying make it very clear you didn't have much involvement. KPIs were involved, you guys missed them (including reporting them one time). Appeal happened, still voted by jury of peers to be allowed to submit. Then the main vote happens and you weren't approved, that's the reality.  

The initial and current goals are to provide funding to projects who are on the verge of self sustainability, and helping them push towards that. While also teaching about KPIs, holding eachother to high levels of professionalism, and providing a space for builders to share and bounce ideas off of. There have not been any deviations there besides the price of ada, which in my opinion made this round the best it's ever been, forcing people to really fight and show value. 

This is certainly not the way I expected the Snek team that i know and love to handle defeat, by blaming the DAO, which is literally 40 other projects with the exact same qualifications to be in the room as you. You are basically just shitting on them and saying you are superior when the group decided otherwise. 

That said, there's no debate the funding was much larger round 1 than round 2 which forced people to really reduce their asks, in my opinion stifling innovation. With no catalyst now, there aren't alot of options. 

At the end of the day, I will always love the Snek team and project and am happy to say that i did infact vote for your proposal, and it ranked even higher on my list after the price reduction. That's the thing about a DAO though, it's not just one persons' decisions and we have to respect the results. If there are changes to be made lets note them and work towards improving for next round.

text:
@AlfonsoSpencer9 The amount of ADA asked without contingency, at an exchange rate of 0.35, does not cover the expected USD amount

Current price is ~0.25

If the entire contingency proposed is allocated solely for the purpose of market volatility, it covers the dollar amount up to ~0.26

If we converted everything into stables from the start we'd receive less funding than the expected cost of realization
author: MicheleHarmonic

text:
@davidtwltr If youre doing generic dev work, thats fine. Any mediocre dev with AI can do that now.

Thats far different than building a financial system where quality matters.

I wont name names, but there are a number of projects in our space that thought they could spend less and get a team of devs outside of the west and still get their new innovative platform delivered.

They were wrong.

The reality is they would have been far better off finding the elite 300k per year dev to build the whole thing for them.

That skill gap has only gotten worse with AI. Mediocre devs still produce mediocre code with AI, because they dont spend the time to truly understand architecture. The elite devs do, so the 10x devs have become 100x devs and are more valuable now than ever.

@planetmaaz @JaromirTesar I would too.

I think theres probably a good way to run a SWF that isnt excessively bloated, but itll require delegation to regulated corporations for that to happen.

text:
@pidnight @JaromirTesar We should follow an exponential decay function for using funds, especially for growth initiatives.

Things like the liquidity budget and CSWF dont spend. They deploy into the ecosystem or are designed to grow the treasury.

Letting the funds sit in the treasury is like sitting on a pile of a billion dollars. With inflation and other activity going on, itll lose value if not put to use somehow.

We should really only target a 3-5 year runway. If we cant be sustainable by then, its not worth having a treasury at all.

text:
@planetmaaz Selecting an alternative node implementation simply because it is “alt” is not sufficient justification for long-term ecosystem investment. What ultimately matters is the strength, reliability, and long-term commitment of the team maintaining it. If an alternative node becomes critical infrastructure once it reaches maturity, it will inevitably require continuous maintenance, security updates, and operational support.

Ecosystems therefore should invest not just in the codebase, but in the teams behind these implementations. Ensuring those teams have the capacity and commitment to maintain the software, respond to issues, and support integrations is essential. This becomes particularly important as businesses begin building on top of these nodes, where reliability, responsiveness, and long-term stewardship directly impact adoption and trust in the infrastructure.

text:
@VKyslik This is a complex topic. Voting power should be an indicator of quality. ADA holders should decide through delegations.

The problem is what to do if the top DReps are FEs and Wallet DReps. This does not necessarily indicate their quality, but the strength of Genesis coins and the laziness of some ADA holders who simply vote for Wallet DReps out of laziness.

Smaller DReps can do better work than the top DReps. They are just unlucky not to be seen. They deserve rewards as well.

Maybe we need to have two pillars for rewards. One would reward proportionally according to voting power, say the top 100 DReps. The other would reward everyone who writes rationales equally, say just the top 500. Maybe we should evaluate the number of delegations, not just the size (but that is not resistant to Sybil attacks).

However, we should pay not only DReps. We need to:

- Pay experts.
- Pay IOG for implementing the saturation limit + other things (they have a budget from last year for this).

text:
Welcome to Nomos 🫡
Cardano's governance is genuinely impressive. But right now? It's a mess to navigate. Proposals buried in PDFs. Rationales scattered across forums. And submitting a governance action costs 100K ADA upfront — meaning most people are locked out before they even start.
We thought that was worth fixing.

WHAT IS NOMOS? 
Nomos is an open-source governance platform for Cardano. Free. Customisable. Built to stay that way.
Our first product is CGOV — live now at https://t.co/EZo1UpGOC5. It pulls everything governance-related into one place so you actually know what's happening on-chain. No wallet needed. No account. Just open it.

WHAT YOU CAN DO TODAY 
→ Browse every active governance action 
→ Track live votes, thresholds & deadlines 
→ Read how DReps, SPOs & CC members voted  and why 
→ See voting power visualised across voters 
→ Download full proposal data as JSON, CSV or MD

WHO BUILT THIS? 
@MeshSDK and @SIDAN_Lab. We crossed paths at the Cardano Summit in Berlin, stayed a few extra days, and hacked the first version together. That was November 2025. Two months later, here we are.
Everything is Apache 2.0. Governance tooling shouldn't sit behind a paywall or depend on one team to keep the lights on. Fork it. Build on it. Make it yours.

WHAT'S COMING
→ Vote on-chain directly from https://t.co/uRYJv0V4Uh (wallet connect) → Submit governance actions — with community crowdfunding for the deposit 
→ Custom analytics dashboards you can share or import 
→ Proposal Forums, CGOV API, Governance MCPs & more
Nomos is just getting started. CGOV is the first chapter.

If you care about Cardano governance — or you're still trying to figure out what it even is — stick around. That's exactly who this is for.
🔔 Follow for weekly governance updates, proposal breakdowns & product news.
Cardano governance, simplified. https://t.co/EZo1UpGOC5  |  https://t.co/JzyvZj7p2r

ONE LAST THING 
🔹We've been heads down building — but we want to hear from you.
🔹What's actually broken about Cardano governance for you right now? 
🔹What made you give up halfway
🔹What would make you come back?
🔹Drop it in the comments. No filter needed.
🔹We read everything. The good ideas end up in the product.
author: Nomos_guild

Cardano 2026年予算サイクルに向けた、新しい予算プロセスの枠組み案（Info Action）が提出されました。

CardanoのTreasury（財務基金）は、エコシステムにとって重要な戦略的リソースです。

今回の提案は、2025年の学びを踏まえつつ、Vision 2030の優先事項や測定可能な成果（KPI）に沿って議論と評価が進むように、2026年の予算策定プロセスを整理することを目的としています。
※この投票は予算の承認や資金配分先の決定ではなく、枠組みを採用するかどうかの意思確認です。

text:
Treasury Withdrawal requesting ₳6.9M for an alternative node called Dingo was submitted at an inopportune time.

For DReps, the decision may be difficult.

Wider diversity brings greater network stability. Cardano should have at least 3 node implementations, ideally evenly used by SPOs (33,3% each node). 

Let's assume they will be willing to install new versions.

The team is capable of delivering.

In these aspects alone, I am in favor of supporting the project.

I hesitate whether the time is right.

The Intersect budget process started last week. I would like DReps to make decisions in a broader context.

We should know how many node implementations are being built and which teams will request funding. What if, in a month, another great team submits a request for funding for another implementation?

Catalyst was recently canceled, leaving builders without support. This means that more teams will consider funding through on-chain governance.

The current NCL is ₳350M. We know that:

▪️A liquidity proposal is due to be submitted soon, asking for ₳50M. Elder was considering ₳100M.

▪️There is talk in the community about the Sovereign Wealth Fund and Pentad V2. This could be another big request around ₳50M to ₳100M (my estimate).

▪️If Catalyst continues, CF will probably ask for tens of millions. Last time it was ₳70M.

▪️Last year, IO asked for ₳96M and ₳27M for maintenance and development of the Haskel node and for research.

These activities alone can consume about 50% to 80% of NCL.

Let's add that the price of ADA is significantly less favorable than last year.

I'm afraid that there won't be enough resources left for other builders. 

That's why I need to have as much context as possible before making a funding decision.

Within a limited budget, we need to support projects that will attract users and have a direct impact on the protocol's revenue.

We need to think about liquidity. CF promised 8-figures, but we haven't had any updates yet.

Node diversity is important, at least for me. 

DReps may find themselves in a situation where they have to choose between diversity and a solid RWA project, L2, or liquidity. That can be a challenge.

As a DRep, I am afraid I don't have the key information to decide on the Dingo proposal right now. 

I would like this proposal to be submitted after the Intersect budget process, or as part of it.

I'm still thinking about it intensively.

I'd appreciate any feedback.

text:
If the 300 million ada NCL doesn’t pass, then imo (not speaking on behalf of the ECC) we won’t have an active NCL.

The new constitution requires the CC to vote on Info GAs, of which the NCL is. This is a departure from the previous constitution, which stated that an Info GA could be neither constitutional nor unconstitutional.

Therefore, as the 350 million NCL Info GA did not meet the threshold for constitutionality, it cannot be considered to be the active NCL.

text:
🇪🇺 Big news for institutional crypto in Europe, but it wasn't easy. 🏦

Cardano Foundation CEO Frederik Gregaard confirms the successful integration of Cardano into the Archax ecosystem, a regulated digital exchange in the UK and EU.

Gregaard described the deal as a "tough one," but it's a major breakthrough for real-world asset (RWA) tokenization. Now, funds like MemberCaps Fund I are placed within Archax's regulated infrastructure, meaning assets issued on Cardano will meet strict European financial standards from day one.

This move is about bridging the gap to institutional liquidity. By gaining direct access to a platform regulated in the UK and Europe, large players can now tokenize traditional assets from real estate to securities on the Cardano blockchain with legal clarity.

Gregaard notes this integration, combined with other projects, creates real business opportunities. Is this the kind of regulatory clarity and adoption that finally brings "big capital" on-chain?

Published: March 8, 2026 | TradingView

#Cardano #ADA #RWATokenization #Archax  #englot #GenoaRoma #HappySUGADAY  #BlockchainNews #EuropeCrypto #LenaMiu #paofc #olypaok

author: SIPO_Tokyo

text:
Thank you for your good proposal. As you know, Cardano is in a bearish market, and in the past, various products have received funding, been completed, but no one has used them. Therefore, I would like to ask a serious question about the demand side.

Q 1: SPO Adoption Demand
Have you conducted any research or surveys targeting SPOs regarding demand for a Go node implementation specifically? If so, where can we review the results? We'd like to focus on demand for a Go node in particular, not alternative node implementations in general. I have personally met SPOs considering Amaru adoption, but I have yet to encounter anyone expressing a desire to run a Go node. What concrete advantages does Dingo offer SPOs over Amaru (Rust) or the Haskell node?

Q 2: Go Developer Contribution Demand
Have you conducted any research into the Go developer community's interest in contributing to Cardano? The proposal states that there are 5 million+ active Go developers, but could you provide evidence for the following chain of logic?
・Go developers actually want to contribute to Cardano node infrastructure
・The absence of a Go node is a meaningful barrier to that contribution
Are both steps of this reasoning supported by actual data or community feedback, rather than inference from ecosystem size alone?
author: yutazzz

text:
💡🌐民主主義4.0：AI × DAO × オンチェーン国家──Cardanoが挑む次の社会制度

民主主義は、国家のものではなくなるかもしれません。

多くの人がまだ気づいていませんが、
ブロックチェーンは「金融」だけでなく「民主主義」も変え始めています。

そしてその議論は、すでに始まっています。

Ethereum共同創業者の Vitalik Buterin 氏が、DAOやAI、ZK投票などを含めて

「これからの民主主義をどう設計すべきか」

という非常に重要な問題提起をしています。

ただ、この議論を理解する上で大事なポイントがあります。

それは
これは既存の民主主義の話ではない
ということです。

民主主義は歴史的に進化してきました。

民主主義1.0
古代ギリシャ型の直接民主制

民主主義2.0
近代国家の代表民主制

民主主義3.0
インターネットによるデジタル民主主義

そして今、議論され始めているのが
民主主義4.0
です。

民主主義4.0の構成要素は
・AI（意思決定の代理）
・DAO（分散型組織）
・ブロックチェーン（ルール実装）
・暗号技術（信頼の担保）
です。

つまり
政治制度そのものがプロトコル化する世界
です。

AIは人間の意思を代理し、
DAOは組織を分散化し、
ブロックチェーンはルールを実装する。

これにより
社会の意思決定がオンチェーン化する
可能性が出てきます。

この視点で見ると、

Cardano が進めている
・オンチェーンガバナンス
・DRep制度
・トレジャリー投票
・憲法
などは
単なるブロックチェーン運営ではなく、
次の時代のデジタル民主制度を前に進める取り組み
とも言えるものです。

もちろん、この意味をまだ十分理解していない人もいますし、既存の政治や民主主義の概念で語ろうとする議論も見かけます。

しかし重要なのは、
今どこで何が起きているのかを正しく見ること
だと思います。

AI × DAO × オンチェーンガバナンス

この組み合わせは、
社会の意思決定の仕組みそのものを大きく変えていく可能性があります。

そしてこの領域で
Cardanoコミュニティは
すでに世界をリードするオンチェーンガバナンスを実装し、次の時代の制度設計を最先端で前に進めているプロトコルと言えるでしょう。

text:
💡🇺🇸🌒米政府が認めた「金融プライバシー」──Cardano Midnightが示す次世代ブロックチェーンの方向

今回の米財務省レポートは、カルダノコミュニティ、とくに Midnight に興味がある人にとってかなり重要なニュースだと思います。

報告書の中で米政府は次の点を明確にしています。

「暗号資産ミキサーは犯罪に使われることがあるが、
合法的な利用者が金融プライバシーを確保するために使う場合もある」

つまり政府自身が
金融プライバシーの正当性
を公式文書で認めた形です。

ここは暗号資産政策としてかなり重要な転換点です。

なぜなら、これまでの議論は
・プライバシー＝犯罪
・匿名＝危険
という単純な構図になりがちだったからです。

しかし今回のレポートは違います。

政府は
プライバシーと規制の両立
という考え方を明確にしています。

そしてここで面白いのが
Midnightの設計思想がまさにこの方向に一致していることです。

MidnightはCardanoのパートナーチェーンとして開発されているプライバシー重視のブロックチェーンですが、特徴は単なる匿名チェーンではないことです。

Midnightは
・ゼロ知識証明（ZK）
・選択的情報開示
・コンプライアンス対応
を前提に設計されています。

つまり
必要なプライバシーは守る
必要な証明は可能
というモデルです。

例えば
・「KYC済み」であること
・「規制条件を満たしている」こと
・「資金が合法である」こと
などを
データを公開せずに証明できる
仕組みです。

ゼロ知識証明は
「事実は証明するが、情報は公開しない」
という暗号技術です。

これによって
プライバシーとコンプライアンスを同時に成立させることができます。

今回の米政府レポートが示している方向は
Privacy vs Compliance
ではなく

Privacy + Compliance

です。

そしてこのモデルをかなり早い段階から研究していたのがCardano / Midnightのアプローチでもあります。

Midnightは
「完全匿名」
でも
「完全監視」
でもない。

その中間、
合理的プライバシー（Rational Privacy）
を目指して設計されています。

これは今後
・金融
・企業
・政府
・RWA
などの領域で重要になる可能性が高いと思います。

今回のニュースは一見すると
「ミキサーの話」
に見えますが、実際にはもっと大きいテーマがあります。

それは
ブロックチェーン時代の金融プライバシーとは何か
という議論です。

そしてその答えの一つとして
Midnightのような
ZKベースのコンプライアンス型プライバシー
はかなり興味深い存在だと思います。

これからこの分野の議論は、確実に増えていきそうですね。

text:
Haven't been posting as much as usual lately. Been trying to stay locked in and get Anvil to the next level.

Trying to create new opportunities us as well as pursue existing ones better. Trying to establish a clear purpose and identity. We are working hard to position ourselves to prepare for enterprise adoption. 

CBDAO has also been keeping me very occupied over the last 2 months. Voting ends in about 12 hours so can't wait to see the results!

Additionally, I have been doing some internal soul searching. Thinking about how I can be a better version of myself and best work towards the legacy I commonly refer to. 

It's a weird time for sure, but I'm always trying my best to be optimistic and see the end goal. 

We push forward.

@Seven0fn9 To provide clarity on those questions, here's an example of how I answered for tew whitepaper idea:

1) What is the core principle you are trying to solve for?
Ensure critical open source infrastructure remains reliably maintained, coordinated, and accountable so that the ecosystem can depend on it over the long term.

2) What is the core behavior you are trying to incentivize?
Incentivize contributors, maintainers, and organizations to actively steward shared infrastructure; continuing maintenance, contributing resources, and taking long-term responsibility for key projects.

text:
During the recent Roundtable Talk on the topic “Understanding the Adoption Gap in Supply Chain Management,” participants explored why interoperability remains a challenge across global supply chains. One point raised during the discussion was that many players in the industry are building their own solutions without relying on existing standards, which often results in systems that are not interoperable.

Here’s @MariekeOFC sharing her perspective on standards, coordination, and why the real issue may not be the absence of standards, but the lack of their consistent use:

“We have a lot of standards. It's a lack of using them, and it's a lack of coordination on the right use. I mean, look at GS1, right. GS1 stands for Global Standard number 1.

If we compare a supply chain like cacao, like cocoa, with a supply chain like juices, the use of standardization is much higher in juice than it is in cacao. Because it's a lot of small holders, a lot of small players, a lot of traders, and little enforcement. So I think we don't need more standards. We need to use them!” 🧵
